_description: Template for recording a project risk entry. Copy this file, replace placeholder values, and save to the appropriate risk category folder under /knowledge_base/risk_library/.
_instructions: 2. Replace all <<PLACEHOLDER>> values with risk analysis data
3. Always link to at least one real reference project where this risk has been observed
4. Use the controlled vocabularies defined in database_architecture.md §6
5. Risk score = Probability (1–5) × Impact (1–5) on a 1–25 scale
6. Risk level: low (1–4), medium (5–9), high (10–16), critical (17–25)
7. Tag cascading risks using their risk_id references
risk_name: <<PEM Electrolyzer Stack Degradation Exceeding Warranty Specifications>>
risk_description: <<PEM electrolyzer stacks may experience accelerated degradation when operated under highly dynamic conditions with frequent start-stop cycles, leading to efficiency loss beyond the OEM warranty curve. This results in higher electricity consumption per kg H₂, reducing project economics. Degradation is typically non-linear and may accelerate after 40,000–50,000 operating hours, well within the project financing period.>>

assessment.assessment_method: <<Expert elicitation workshop with 5 SMEs from electrolyzer OEMs, project developers, and independent engineers. Delphi method, 2 rounds.>>
assessment.assessed_by: <<Internal Risk Assessment Team — Green Hydrogen Copilot Project>>

consequences.description: <<If stack degradation exceeds the warranted rate (typically ~1%/year for PEM), the project faces: (1) Progressive increase in electricity consumption per kg H₂ — a 2%/year degradation vs 1% warranted means ~10% higher electricity cost by year 10. (2) Early stack replacement at year 5–6 instead of year 8–10, adding €15–20M unplanned CAPEX for a 100 MW plant. (3) Potential disputes with OEM over warranty coverage if degradation is attributed to operational profile. (4) Reduced plant availability during stack replacement (4–6 weeks per module replacement).>>
consequences.worst_case_scenario: <<Catastrophic stack failure requiring full replacement at year 3. OEM disputes warranty due to operational profile (frequent starts). Unbudgeted €20M+ replacement cost. 3 months downtime. Project IRR drops 3–5 percentage points, potentially triggering loan covenant breach.>>

actions.description: <<Include stack degradation warranty with clear operational profile boundaries in EPC contract. Negotiate degradation guarantee: ≤1.0%/year for first 5 years, ≤1.5%/year through year 10.>>
<<Implement advanced condition monitoring system: individual cell voltage monitoring, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) periodic testing, AI-based degradation prediction.>>
<<Design plant with N+1 stack redundancy so one module can be offline for maintenance without stopping production. Size BOP for full capacity even with one module down.>>
<<Establish stack replacement sinking fund: allocate €0.05/kg H₂ produced into reserved account starting year 1. By year 8, fund covers 100% of stack replacement cost.>>

incident_descriptions: <<Project Alpha (Germany, 100 MW PEM): Stack degradation of 1.8%/year observed in first 3 years vs 1.0% warranted. Root cause: higher-than-expected start-stop cycles due to wind variability. OEM provided partial compensation. Replacement advanced to year 5.>>

<<Project Beta (Netherlands, 20 MW PEM): Membrane thinning detected at 35,000 hours via EIS. Preventive stack replacement at 40,000 hours — 33% earlier than planned. Learning: EIS monitoring is effective for early detection.>>
evidence.lessons_learned: <<(1) Real-world degradation often exceeds lab/pilot data due to dynamic operation. (2) OEM warranties typically exclude degradation caused by 'excessive cycling' — operational profile must be negotiated upfront. (3) EIS monitoring provides 12–18 months early warning before irreversible degradation. (4) Stack replacement fund is essential — do not depend solely on warranty. (5) N+1 redundancy adds ~8% CAPEX but eliminates production loss during stack swaps.>>
evidence.frequency_in_reference_projects: <<Observed in 3 of 18 reference PEM projects >20 MW with more than 3 years of operating data. Frequency: ~17%.>>
industry_standards_reference: IEC 62282-8-101: Energy storage systems using fuel cell modules
ISO 22734: Hydrogen generators using water electrolysis

sources.title: <<IEA Electrolyser Durability and Degradation: Lessons from First Commercial Plants, 2025>>
<<Accelerated stress tests for PEM electrolysis: Bridging lab and field degradation — Journal of Power Sources, 2024>>

rag_metadata.text_for_embedding: PEM Electrolyzer Stack Degradation Exceeding Warranty Specifications. Category: technical > Electrolyzer Performance. Probability: moderate, Impact: major. Applies to: PEM at medium_10_to_100mw. Consequences: Accelerated stack degradation beyond warranty rate leads to higher electricity consumption, early stack replacement costs, OEM warranty disputes, and production downtime. Mitigation strategy: reduce. Evidence: Observed in 3 of 18 reference PEM projects >20 MW with >3 years data (~17% frequency). Lessons: Real-world degradation often exceeds lab data; EIS monitoring provides early warning; stack replacement fund essential. Keywords: PEM, stack degradation, warranty, dynamic operation, efficiency loss, stack replacement.
